title: Digital certificates
description: Check your understanding of digital certificates and certificate authorities in this set of free practice questions designed for AP Computer Science Principles students.

assessment_items.question_content:
Nombeko is creating a social messaging app to use with her friends. She registers the domain "friendzers.com" to host the backend for the app. She then signs up for a digital certificate from a certificate authority and installs the certificate on the domain.

**What does the certificate prove?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The certificate proves that "friendzers.com" and the associated public key are owned by the same entity.
The certificate proves that "friendzers.com" is following all security best practices with user data.
The certificate proves that "friendzers.com" is a legally operated company in the United States.
The certificate proves that "friendzers.com" maps to the associated IP address.

assessment_items.question_content:
Mr. Jones bought a server where his students can upload homework assignments and mapped the domain "writing302.org" to it. He then acquired a digital certificate for "writing302.org" from a certificate authority and installed it on the server.

**What does the certificate prove?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The certificate proves that "writing302.org" and its associated encryption key are both owned by the same entity.
The certificate proves that "writing302.org" is certified as compliant with US data protection laws.
The certificate proves that "writing302.org" is a certified non-profit corporation.
The certificate proves that "writing302.org" maps to the associated IP address.

assessment_items.question_content:
Paula is creating a journalism organization to do investigative reporting. She registers the domain "whistleblowerz.org" and signs up with a hosting company to provide an email server, so that the journalists can easily communicate with each other.

She's debating whether to acquire a digital certificate for her domain from a certificate authority.

**What benefit would the certificate bring?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The certificate for "whistleblowerz.org" would associate a public key with the domain, and enable the server to use TLS for secure email sending.
The certificate for "whistleblowerz.org" would verify to users that the associated news stories are not "fake news".
The certificate for "whistleblowerz.org" would ensure that the journalists using the email server all use strong passwords.
The certificate would notify the domain name servers that "mail.whistleblowerz.org" maps to the associated IP address for the email server.

assessment_items.question_content:
On September 11, 2017, the Google Chrome security team announced a plan to stop trusting certificates issued from the Symantec certificate authority. The Chrome team announced the plan after reports that some of Symantec's certificate issuing organizations were issuing certificates to domains that weren't properly verified.

**Why is it so important that the Google Chrome team monitors the trustworthiness of certificates?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: If a cybercriminal acquires a certificate for a domain they don't own, they can use that to secretly steal private data from that domain's website users.

A certificate indicates a domain is following best practices for securing user data. If a certificate isn't properly verified, then Chrome users might accidentally use websites that don't handle their data well.
If a domain doesn't go through the proper verification steps for the certificate, then their domain → IP mapping may not work correctly, and users won't be able to load the domain in Chrome.
If a cybercriminal can register a domain for a certificate without verifying it, then they can register domains for activities that are illegal in some countries.

assessment_items.question_content:
In 2011, the Dutch certificate authority DigiNotar was infiltrated by a cybercriminal. The attacker managed to create hundreds of false certificates, including ones for popular domains like google.com and facebook.com. 

When the attack was publicized, companies like Apple and Microsoft blacklisted DigiNotar in their lists of trusted certificate authorities.

**Why was it important for the DigiNotar certificates to be blacklisted?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The attacker could launch a DNS spoofing attack on the certificates' domains and then intercept private data from the domains' users.
The attacker could sell the false certificates for thousands of dollars, raising the costs of certificates unnecessarily.
The attacker could use the false certificates to spread viruses on the web servers of the compromised domains.
The attacker could use the false certificates to gain access to the databases of the domains and download all their previously stored data.

assessment_items.question_content:
In 2011, the certificate authority GlobalSign reported that a cybercriminal managed to hack into their user-facing website. 

Upon discovery, GlobalSign stopped issuing new certificates and asked a security company to investigate whether the cybercriminal had infiltrated their certificate issuing infrastructure as well. They needed to make sure the cybercriminal did not make any false certificates during the attack.

**Why was it important that GlobalSign took steps to prevent the creation of false certificates?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The cybercriminal could create false certificates linking other organization's domains to their own private key, and then use those certificates during DNS spoofing attacks.
The cybercriminal could create false certificates for popular domains and sell them to those domains for exorbitant amounts of money.
The cybercriminal could create false certificates for other organization's domains, and use those certificates to access the databases used by those organizations.
The cybercriminal could create false certificates for popular domains, and use those certificates to replace the domains with their own illegal website.

assessment_items.question_content:
Johannes learns about certificate authorities and is concerned that he doesn't know which authorities to trust when browsing the web.

**What is good advice for Johannes?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: "Don't worry, there's only one certificate authority that you should trust, and it's easy to memorize one name."
"If you trust your browser, then you don't need to worry about exactly which certificate authorities to trust."
"If you trust the website that you're visiting, then it doesn't matter whether you trust the certificate authority of their certificate."
"You'll have to decide which certificate authority to trust, but at least you can follow a standard checklist."

assessment_items.question_content:
Aliza visited a website over HTTPS and saw a lock icon in the URL bar, indicating a secure connection with a valid digital certificate.

**Which of these statements describes the trust model in this scenario?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Aliza trusts the browser, the browser trusts the certificate authority, and the certificate authority trusts the website.
The browser trusts Aliza, Aliza trusts the certificate authority, and the certificate authority trusts the website.
The certificate authority trusts Aliza, Aliza trusts the browser, and the browser trusts the website.
Aliza trusts the certificate authority, the certificate authority trusts the browser, and the browser trusts the website.

assessment_items.question_content:
Secure web browsing relies on a trust model to verify the authenticity of digital certificates.

**Which of these best describe the trust model involved?**

_An arrow from one entity to another signifies that the first entity trusts the second._

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: User → Browser → Certificate Authority → Website
User → Certificate Authority →  Browser → Website
Certificate authority → Browser → User → Website
Certificate authority → User → Browser → Website

assessment_items.question_content:
**When connecting securely to a website, what information must be in their digital certificate?**

👁️**Note that there are 2 answers to this question.**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The website's public key
The website's private key
The website's domain name
The website's admin email address

assessment_items.question_content:
Melodie connected securely with HTTPS to this website:

https:\/\/www.farm-fresh-csa.com/customize

Her browser validated the digital certificate of the website before loading the page.

**Which table is most representative of the contents of the digital certificate?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content:
Field | Value
- | - | -
**Domain name** | *.farm-fresh-csa.com
**Public key** | MIGeMA0GCSqGSIb3DQEBAQUAA
**Certificate authority** | DST Root CA X3

Field | Value
- | - | -
**Domain name** | *.farm-fresh-csa.com
**Private key** | MIGeMA0GCSqGSIb3DQEBAQUAA
**Certificate authority** | DST Root CA X3

Field | Value
- | - | -
**Full path** | www.farm-fresh-csa.com/customize
**Public key** | MIGeMA0GCSqGSIb3DQEBAQUAA
**Certificate authority** | DST Root CA X3

Field | Value
- | - | -
**Full path** | www.farm-fresh-csa.com/customize
**Private key** | MIGeMA0GCSqGSIb3DQEBAQUAA
**Certificate authority** | DST Root CA X3

assessment_items.question_content:
Madden connected securely with HTTPS to this website:

https:\/\/www.football-gamers.com/teams/tigers

His  browser validated the digital certificate of the website before loading the page.

The digital certificate contained this encryption key:

```
MIGfMA0GCSqGSIb3DQEBAQUAA4GNADCBiQKBgQCIU3Ry71RkLpMgZxgnxHJNopUn
4WSPAGdPv6f9K06uNwJ0oLWo8pbN1AP08akXmR/DS4XG0QUpfSSWPeJrhaUhkODn
5RKpQtEevwBNC6enOwcPTZCzT9nRvd57L+vHFFfQ0iojHLccDl8in/nhbAvUg+lO
uumApeh1tzs5feYe3QIDAQAB
```

**What type of key is that and who does it belong to?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It's the private key of football-gamers.com
It's the public key of football-gamers.com
It's the private key of Madden's browser
It's the public key of Madden's browser
